title: How Women Can Build Wealth Through Real Estate: Creative Deals & Tax Strategies
guestBio: Dave Lundgren is a real estate investor and bestselling author of Loveness who specializes in creative financing strategies and lease options. He helps investors navigate today's challenging real estate market through alternative investment approaches that deliver higher returns than traditional rental properties.
excerpt: Traditional real estate investing isn't working for most women — but creative financing strategies like lease options are delivering 59% total returns. Dave Lundgren shares the insider tactics and tax loopholes that can transform your wealth-building strategy.
metaDescription: Learn creative real estate investing strategies for women, including lease options, tax loopholes, and financing deals without traditional bank loans.
keyTakeaways: Traditional rental properties are failing to cashflow in today's market, but lease options can deliver 59% total returns.
Section 168-179 of the IRS code is a hidden loophole that can potentially eliminate your entire tax bill through real estate investments.
Creative financing allows you to purchase property without traditional bank loans, opening up more opportunities for wealth building.
Women's natural relationship capital is an underutilized advantage in real estate investing that can lead to better deals and partnerships.
Understanding the difference between active and passive investing is crucial for choosing strategies that fit your lifestyle and goals.
Proper evaluation of deals, teams, and syndications requires specific metrics and due diligence before investing any money.
Real estate remains one of the most effective paths to generational wealth when approached with the right strategies and knowledge.

pullQuotes.text: Women's relationship capital is the most underused advantage in real estate
Lease options deliver 59% total returns while traditional rentals are failing to cashflow

quiz.title: What's Your Real Estate Investment Style?
quiz.description: Discover which real estate investing approach aligns with your personality, lifestyle, and wealth-building goals.

A.name: Creative Deal Maker
A.description: You thrive on finding unique opportunities and building relationships that lead to win-win scenarios. Your natural problem-solving abilities and people skills make you perfect for creative financing strategies like lease options and seller financing. You're not afraid of complexity when it leads to better returns. This episode will give you the specific strategies and language you need to start structuring deals that others can't see.

B.name: Strategic Wealth Builder
B.description: You prefer to leverage your existing income and expertise rather than learning entirely new skill sets. Your analytical mind and risk management approach make you ideal for carefully vetted passive investments and syndications. You understand that time is your most valuable asset and want your money to work as hard as you do. This episode will help you identify the right opportunities and ask the right due diligence questions.

C.name: Relationship-Focused Investor
C.description: Your superpower is building trust and understanding what people really need. You excel at creating partnerships and finding mutually beneficial solutions that traditional approaches miss. Your natural networking abilities and emotional intelligence are exactly what today's real estate market rewards. This episode will show you how to turn your relationship capital into investment opportunities.

D.name: Tax-Smart Optimizer
D.description: You're already successful but want to keep more of what you earn while building long-term wealth. Your detail-oriented approach and understanding of financial strategy make you perfect for maximizing tax advantages through real estate. You see investing not just as wealth building but as wealth protection. This episode will reveal the specific tax strategies and loopholes that can transform your financial picture.

questions.question: When you hear about a potential investment opportunity, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Start brainstorming creative ways to structure the deal
Research the market fundamentals and projected returns
Want to meet the people involved and understand their motivations
Calculate how it would impact your tax situation and overall portfolio

questions.question: How do you prefer to spend your time on wealth-building activities?

options.text: Actively finding and negotiating deals, even if it's complex
Researching and vetting opportunities created by others
Building relationships and networks that lead to opportunities
Optimizing and strategizing around existing investments and income

questions.question: What excites you most about real estate investing?

options.text: The challenge of solving problems that others can't or won't tackle
The predictable cash flow and long-term wealth building potential
The ability to help people while building your own wealth
The tax advantages and portfolio diversification benefits

questions.question: How do you typically make important financial decisions?

options.text: Trust your instincts and adapt as you learn more
Thoroughly analyze data and compare multiple options
Seek input from trusted advisors and consider all stakeholders
Consider long-term strategic implications and tax consequences

questions.question: What's your biggest concern about real estate investing?

options.text: Not finding deals that are creative or profitable enough
Choosing the wrong investment and losing money
Working with people who don't share your values
Missing tax optimization opportunities or regulatory changes

questions.question: When you imagine your ideal real estate investment success, what does it look like?

options.text: Building a reputation as someone who can structure deals others can't
Having a diversified portfolio generating predictable passive income
Creating a network of trusted partners and mutually beneficial relationships
Minimizing your tax burden while maximizing long-term wealth accumulation

content:
If you've been watching the real estate market and feeling like you missed the boat, I have news for you: the traditional playbook isn't working for anyone right now. But that doesn't mean the wealth-building opportunities have disappeared — it means you need to start thinking differently about how you approach real estate investing.

In this powerful conversation with real estate investor and author Dave Lundgren, we dive deep into why women are still missing out on the deals that build generational wealth, and more importantly, what you can do about it starting today. This isn't about waiting for market conditions to improve or saving up the perfect down payment. This is about understanding the creative strategies that successful investors are using right now while everyone else is sitting on the sidelines.

Why Traditional Real Estate Investing Is Broken

Let's start with the reality check that most real estate gurus won't tell you: traditional rental properties aren't cashflowing in today's market. With property prices at historic highs and interest rates creating additional pressure, the old formula of buying a rental property and collecting monthly rent simply isn't delivering the returns it used to. This is why so many women who tried to get into real estate investing over the past few years have felt frustrated and defeated.

But here's where Dave's expertise becomes invaluable. Instead of accepting poor returns or giving up entirely, he's pioneered strategies around lease options that are delivering 59% total returns even in this challenging environment. A lease option isn't just a different way to structure a deal — it's a completely different approach to real estate that gives you more control, better cash flow, and significantly higher returns than traditional rentals.

The beauty of lease options is that they allow you to control property without the massive capital requirements of traditional purchases. You're not just collecting rent and hoping for appreciation; you're creating multiple income streams from a single property while building equity in ways that traditional landlording simply can't match.

The Tax Strategy That Changes Everything

One of the most eye-opening parts of our conversation centers on Section 168-179 of the IRS code — a legal loophole that can potentially wipe out your entire tax bill through strategic real estate investments. This isn't about sketchy tax avoidance schemes; this is about understanding and utilizing legal strategies that the wealthy have been using for decades.

The key insight here is that real estate investing isn't just about the returns you generate — it's about how much of those returns you actually get to keep after taxes. When you understand how to structure your investments to take advantage of depreciation, cost segregation studies, and other legal tax strategies, you're not just building wealth through your investments; you're protecting the wealth you're already earning from your other income sources.

This is where the conversation becomes particularly relevant for high-earning women. If you're already successful in your career or business, you're likely paying significant taxes on that income. Real estate investing, when done strategically, can offset those taxes while simultaneously building long-term wealth. It's not either-or; it's a both-and strategy that compounds your existing success.

Your Relationship Capital Is Your Secret Weapon

Here's something that struck me deeply in our conversation: women's natural relationship capital is one of the most underutilized advantages in real estate investing. While men often approach real estate as a numbers game or a competition, women tend to excel at building the relationships and trust that make creative deals possible.

Real estate investing, especially at the creative financing level, is fundamentally about relationships. It's about finding property owners who need solutions, building trust with potential partners, and creating win-win scenarios that traditional bank financing can't accommodate. These are skills that many women already possess but haven't recognized as investable assets.

Think about it: your ability to listen, understand someone's real needs, and craft solutions that work for everyone involved — these aren't soft skills in real estate investing. These are the hard skills that separate successful investors from those who struggle to find deals. Your natural inclination to build relationships rather than just complete transactions is exactly what today's real estate market rewards.

Active vs. Passive: Choosing Your Investment Style

One of the most important distinctions Dave makes is between active and passive real estate investing, and understanding which approach actually fits your life and goals. Active investing means you're finding deals, managing properties, and directly involved in the day-to-day operations. Passive investing means you're providing capital to syndications or funds where someone else handles the operations.

Neither approach is inherently better, but choosing the wrong one for your situation can lead to frustration and poor results. If you're already running a business or managing a demanding career, jumping into active real estate investing without understanding the time commitment can become overwhelming quickly. Conversely, if you have the time and interest to be more hands-on, passive investing might not give you the returns or control you're looking for.

The key is honest self-assessment about your available time, your risk tolerance, and your learning preferences. Some women thrive on the challenge of finding and structuring creative deals; others prefer to leverage their earned income into carefully vetted passive investments. Both paths can build significant wealth when executed properly.

Due Diligence That Actually Works

Whether you're evaluating an active deal or a passive investment opportunity, having a framework for due diligence is crucial. This isn't about becoming a real estate expert overnight; it's about knowing the right questions to ask and the red flags to watch for before you invest a single dollar.

For passive investments, you need to understand the track record of the operators, the market fundamentals of the area, and the realistic projections for returns. Too many investors get caught up in projected returns without understanding the assumptions behind those projections. For active deals, you need to understand comparable sales, renovation costs, and exit strategies before you commit.

The goal isn't to eliminate all risk — that's impossible in any investment. The goal is to understand the risks you're taking and ensure you're being compensated appropriately for those risks. This is where having mentors, joining investment groups, and continuing your education becomes invaluable.

Real estate investing isn't just about building wealth; it's about building the kind of wealth that creates options and freedom in your life. Whether you're looking to supplement your current income, build a retirement fund, or create generational wealth for your family, the strategies and insights from this conversation give you a roadmap to start making smarter moves with your money today.
transcript:
**Aimee:** Today's episode is part two with my friend, Dave Lundgren. Dave has spent decades in the world of business, leadership, and investing alongside some of the most influential minds in personal development and entrepreneurship, including Tony Robbins and Dean DeGrazio. He has helped entrepreneurs scale businesses, think bigger, and build wealth intentionally.

In our first conversation, we explored mindset and leadership, but today we're diving into something very practical, real estate investing. For many Americans, real estate has been one of the most reliable paths to building long-term wealth. But it's not as simple as buying a property and hoping it goes up in value. The real pros understand the metrics, the strategy, and the discipline required to do it well.

Personally, I've been out of the real estate game for a while, except for our home, which thankfully we did very well. But I know real estate can be a powerful tool when it's approached thoughtfully and strategically. So today we're going to unpack the fundamentals, what makes a good investment, how to analyze deals, what types of properties are performing well today, and what someone should know before jumping in.

If you've ever thought about investing in real estate, whether it's single family homes, multifamily storage units, or even mobile home parks, this episode is going to give you a fantastic starting point. Dave, welcome back to The Manager. It's nice to have you.

**Dave:** Thanks, Amy. It's good to see you again.

**Aimee:** It's so good to have you back. Before we dive into strategy, Dave, I'd love to start with your personal journey. How did you first get involved in real estate investing?

**Dave:** Great question. So 25 years ago, I left the army as a chaplain and came into what I would call the high ticket coaching space. So selling real estate as an investment. And you mentioned Dean Graziosi. I worked with him. I was his right hand for years doing the infomercials back in the day when those things existed. And so I really started selling real estate education first. And then I really didn't start buying rentals till a few years after that.

Then as the infomercials wound down, Dean came to me and said, hey, we're moving to live events. We're going to be doing seminars, but you're in Kansas City, Missouri, which is a great market. Take my money and let's do what we call turnkey, which we can explain, but basically selling a rental as an investment to people, to his students.

And so I went from having bought a couple of rentals to doing, we did 200 flips, buy, fix, and flips within 18 months. So I dove in hardcore probably about 2012, 2013, and did a lot of real estate from that point. I became an active investor because of that path.

**Aimee:** So that's a great explanation. What a fun time to jump in because the market was still pretty down at that point from the recession that we went through in 2009. We were still just barely starting to recover in 2012 and 2013. I love real estate. I'm always watching the market. The market that we're in right now, is it a cautious market for buying real estate? What do you think? What are you feeling right now?

**Dave:** Yeah, it's a great question. What I used to think there was one national real estate market. The reality is it's kind of regional. So there are massive things that are large things that influence the entire market, right? Interest rates. And certainly in 2008, there was a trifecta of things happening that affected entire market, but a lot of times since then, it's been a regional thing.

But by and large interest rates have been up. And so things have tightened up and it's become difficult for turnkey real estate or what I would call rentals, passive investing to work. It's been kind of a bit of a challenge and we're going to talk about some of the solutions to that but certainly as you talked about, if you're buying low, 2008 to 2012, and you're able to rehab that and get a good tenant in there and just ride that appreciation.

But back to the regional comment, there's a difference between California where you're at and Kansas City. So even those different markets, there's an affordability sometimes in the Midwest. You can still make real estate work, but I would say it's definitely more of a challenge than it was even 10 years ago. A lot of people got in 10 years ago, oh, this is easy. And the last two years, they're like, oh, we're having some real challenges we didn't anticipate before, so it's been interesting to watch.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I mean, I jumped into real estate in 2002 and you really couldn't make a mistake at that time. It was pretty freaking easy. It didn't matter what crappy house I bought in a good neighborhood. As long as I can make it cute, I could make money. And it was so much fun. I loved it.

And people always come to me and ask me for real estate advice because I did spend a decade of my life flipping houses and owning real estate and making a good amount of money doing it. But surely the market has changed and the savvy investors are going to do well. You can't just jump in and not make a mistake. You could jump in and make a mistake. So being educated is going to be more important now.

And also, like you're saying, regional like California, the cap rates here suck. It's hard to invest your money here and know that you're going to do really well. You're going to do well with the inflation. We're going to have inflation. So the prices of houses are going to go up, but it's not the passive income that we learned from Rich Dad, Poor Dad and Carlton Sheets. And back in those days you could buy something and make some money on it every month.

**Dave:** Yeah. And you brought up a key word. So passive versus active. So real estate investing in a very general sense, and there's a lot of different pieces to this, but there's active real estate investing and passive real estate investing.

Even the great majority of folks that we sold education and training to, they thought they wanted to be active, right? They saw the HGTV. In fact, we worked with Amy and Scott Yancey on the Flipping Vegas. And people see that and they're like, oh, I want to go flip a house. And you did some flipping. And that's fine. And you can make a lot of money doing that. But it does require work. That's active. You're in there. You're doing stuff.

And then there's passive, which is I don't want to swing a hammer at all. I just want to place my capital. And we found that a lot of folks that actually invested a lot of money in education and training realized, oh, this is actually work and there's a job. I just want the benefits.

And so I've done a lot of active investing, but the great majority of what I do now is more that passive side, which is, I just want the benefits of real estate. I don't want to have to go and be active with it. Now, some people are creative. But in a very general sense, people need to decide I'm willing to go put in the work. I'm willing to put some time and effort and make it a business or take my money and go place it somewhere else. And I want to return. That's a very broad statement, but I think people need to decide which category they want to be in from an active versus passive side.

**Aimee:** That is such, such a good point, Dave. That's really the crux of it. And as I'm older now, I'd love to get into passive and I'm looking at a lot of properties and there's absolutely nothing that is passive income. It's amazing. You put money in and you lose money every month.

So it's finding the deals. I think that's another big part of real estate investing is that you're going to have to spend time looking for the right deal. You don't swing at every ball. In tennis, it's all about when you're doing your serve, it's the toss. If the toss is good, then you hit the ball. But if the toss is not good, you don't hit the ball. Don't take the serve or you're in trouble.

**Dave:** And to your point, even within that, there's a lot of great training and education and people can decide, hey, I'm going to go create a system and I want to figure out how to get even in today's market. How can I get something undervalued or I just want to rely on another team to do that. And I've gravitated towards more of that, meaning my partners and I, we've done thousands of deals over the last 20 years.

And people that have decided, I don't want a job. OK, you guys are in five different markets. You've done thousands of deals. You can document nine years, as I mentioned via text. Now we've got 2,100 investors that we've documented. What kind of returns they're getting? We've created a system. And some people are like, OK, as long as I can, just like a stock. If I'm looking at a stock to buy a stock, I'm doing the research on that company. I've decided, okay, Coca-Cola has good foundation, good fundamentals in every market. Same thing with real estate. You can research the market. You can research the team and decide, okay, I don't want to have to go and deploy all these systems. If I can get somebody else to do that that's done that for decades, thousands of examples, I want to create or I want to invest into a system like that.

And that's ultimately where I've gravitated for the same reasons you brought up because the market's so difficult right now. And even with rentals, rentals are difficult. Even in the Midwest, in Kansas City, there's been a crunch on cashflow. So what we've gravitated towards is actually lease options.

And that simply means a rental is just a lease, right? I'm leasing a house. A lease option gives the tenant a lease, but they also get an option to purchase. Well, why do that? Well, the quick answer is instead of getting maybe 100, 200 a month cashflow on a lease option, because of the money they're putting down and because we're charging a premium on rent, because we're not responsible for maintenance. For all these reasons, instead of getting a very minimal cash on cash, we're averaging about 14% cash on cash and a total return when you factor everything in is about 59% on your money.

And people see that and they're like, oh my goodness, all day long, I want to do a lease option versus a rental. So that's one strategy that we're deploying to help people with the cash flow because they're like, yeah, rentals don't make a lot of sense right now. Well, if we can get you $1,000 a month versus $200 a month, all of a sudden that changes the game for folks.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I remember this was the game even back in 2002. We were talking about the lease, the option to purchase with a lease with an option to buy. And you're basically giving your tenant the option to lock in the purchase price. That's the benefit to the tenant. And then the benefit to you is you're going to get some cash up front and you could basically hand over the maintenance of the property to the tenant, which is pretty cool.

**Dave:** For sure. And I've done, I'm actually sitting in a townhouse that's going to be my son's. I got a special needs kid. This is going to be his townhouse. And I've done a lease option on this house more than once.

And to your point, lease options have been around a long time. And it's usually been just a local market thing. Okay, I can do a lease option. It's better than a rental. But there's never been a national solution up until my partners and I came up. We got some software now. And we actually have a national property management. So now we can scale this just like a rental, because it's always been a good strategy, but it always hasn't been able to be systematized nationally.

But you're right. You get a better tenant and about 20% of the time they actually buy the house, right? So 80% of the time they're in there, they act like an owner, they're taking care of everything. They've given you some extra money, higher rents, a deposit up front and yet they end up moving out, which is fine. The 20% they buy, they're buying at retail and you're still getting your cash flow so there's a lot of reasons why those lease options make sense from I just want to own and hold one piece of property as compared to rentals.

And obviously there's other types of passive investing outside of that, but that's one way in the marketplace that we're seeing really good returns. The other thing is looking at trends, at a national level, okay, affordable housing, that's a real issue, right? So ADUs have been doing some of that in Texas or tiny homes or RV parks or mobile homes. That's getting in front of this trend of, okay, affordable housing's a real issue. How do we solve for that? So that'd be the other caveat I would give somebody who's just historically looking at, okay, where are the trends? Affordable housing is a real issue. How can I get in front of that growth and how can I help solve that problem?

And you mentioned storage. I mean, anybody can research self-storage. I got my best friend owns 13 self-storage units. He's done very well for himself, but historically it's the lowest default rate of any type of real estate. The challenge is, it takes some cash to get into them right there millions of dollars and it takes some financing, but that's where syndications come into play where you can invest with other people and be part of it, maybe a fifty hundred thousand dollars or more. And then you can invest as a group.

So self-storage historically has been an amazing investment for some of those same high-level demographics you look at. People are mobile, people are moving, divorce, moving. That's what creates self-storage opportunities because people are moving in and out. So yeah, there's a lot of different types of investment, even on the passive side. We can deploy your capital, research the team, make sure they've got a good history.

And again, I'm very, very biased. Real estate historically, when done right with the right team and the right markets, in the right type of investment, you just can't beat it. You got four returns for every dollar you put in. You don't get that with stocks. You don't get that with gold. You don't get that with commodities. Historically has created more millionaires than any other asset class for sure.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And the tax benefits for real estate, because of the risk that we're taking as real estate investors, the government basically has written the tax laws to really benefit owners, property owners. And so the tax benefits that go to real estate investors are some of the best ones out there, business owners and real estate owners.

Talked about syndicates. Now, my brain is, you got me turning on this one. I'm thinking, okay, if you're interested in investing in something like storage units and you want to get involved in a syndicate of people who are going to be buying together, how do you find people like that? Where do you go?

**Dave:** Yeah, it's a great question. Start clicking on those ads and I promise you there's going to be people advertising to you. But I'm kidding. There's national groups. BiggerPockets is a free website that's out there that has a lot of free information. Now, the challenge with that is sometimes free information isn't that awesome or great.

Anybody that reaches out to me, I'll give you my two cents on all sorts of different syndications that exist, some that I'm not a part of, right? Because I only want to work with people that want to work with me and have the specific same goals and same risk-reward balance, things that I'm looking at, right? Because some people are in a different stage of life. An 80 year old couple that is using all their retirement money. That's a different conversation than a 25 year old dude who's got a high risk, high reward.

So I'll shoot anybody straight say okay where are you at? Where's your risk reward? What kind of returns you're looking for? And then based on that you can make some smart decisions. So you could research, I mean, you can go into chat GPT and research real estate syndications. And then again, looking at the asset class, okay. Multifamily. Does that make sense to commercials, self-storage, a syndication of several people buying single family houses together, development. We do a lot of new construction development.

So it's going to take some research. And there are groups out there that do provide training and education specific to past investing. Just like on the active side, they'll educate you, they'll train you and say, okay, based on your goals, this is the type of real estate asset you should get into. And then syndications are, I mean, it's really something that's exploded in the last 20 years, but it really, it kind of started with a bunch of people hanging out at the golf club and there's the doctor, there's a lawyer, there's a local county dude, and there's the realtor, and they've had a conversation at the golf course about, oh, hey, I'm going to put my money into real estate.

Well, that has grown and grown as alternative investments have grown. And now it's become a huge marketplace where people are like, okay, I want the benefits of real estate. I don't want to have to manage it. Let me go find a new group. And at the end of the day, that's what a syndication is, is you're just placing your faith and trust in a group that has a history of they're going to go do the work, your capital, their expertise, their time, their money as well. But that's what a syndication is for those folks who have never invested in one. It's like buying a stock, except you're buying into a real estate development or a real estate project.

**Aimee:** Nice. Are they still called REITs, R-E-I-T-S, a real estate investment trust? Or what are they called now, the vehicle?

**Dave:** Yeah, that's a great question. So REIT is similar. But actually a REIT is that the legal structure is different. So on a fund or syndication, there's all sorts of different legal structures, 506C, 506D. And depending on who they're marketing to, you can only market some investments to accredited investor, right? So somebody who's made $300,000 or has a net worth of a million, ironically, as crappy as this is, they get access to some investments that a non-accredited investor don't get.

A REIT is just a legal structure that's actually distinct from a syndication. It's literally, again, just like buying a stock, but it's just a different legal structure. But it's the same intended outcome where you're just buying a piece of a share. Say there's a hundred shares in a company, right? You're buying stock. A REIT, you get ownership in that. And it's the same concept. Somebody else is managing your money, investing into, and they have to disclose just like these other funds and stuff, they have to disclose, here's exactly what you're getting into. We're investing into multifamily assets. Indianapolis by fix and flip or whatever. So it's just a different legal structure, but very similar outcome at a high level.

**Aimee:** That's the very, are you looking at quarterly dividends on those kinds of investments? Is that how it pays out?

**Dave:** Great question. They're all different. So some pay monthly. You can get involved in the syndication who they don't pay you for two years because it's maybe a development thing. And if you look at they call it internal rate of return, which is a very complicated formula. But typically, it's going to be a combination of a monthly cash flow or a quarterly dividend cash flow and then an equity piece.

Some only pay cash flow, some pay only equity, and some do a blend. And so by equity, I mean, okay, we're going to go develop something, we're going to fix it up, and then we're going to have an exit and sell it off. You can sometimes participate in that equity.

And as you do your research at a very broad level, that internal rate of return in the marketplace, I'm not talking about you find a killer deal in your back pocket. I'm talking about, generally speaking, most syndications, they're getting somewhere between 20% to 29% internal rate of return. Meaning when you look at the cash flow and you look at the exit, again, depending on the risk reward, that's generally where the marketplace is for somebody that just wants to be passive and place their capital into something. That's where the market is right.

**Aimee:** Wow. That's really interesting, Dave. And you mentioned the guys on the golf course. And this is one of the conversations that I have a lot on this show is that we as women are missing out on these financial opportunities because we're not on the golf course having these conversations with our girlfriends. We're not at brunch talking about money and how to make money. It should be, it should be, but it's so loud in there sometimes it's hard to talk with all the blow dryers going and all that stuff.

But I don't know why we're not talking about these and having these conversations, but it makes me think, if it was something that people wanted, could the manager have its own real estate syndicate for investment? Is that something that you would help me build out?

**Dave:** A hundred percent. Yeah. I'd love to do that. And I love that what you're doing, teaching women entrepreneurship and leadership. And certainly for me, I mean, I love real estate as a passive vehicle, but at the end of the day, it's about that freedom and flexibility, which is important. I know to your listeners, to you based on that.

Right. As your listeners are doing their businesses or whatever they're doing to make money, if we can take some of that capital and place it and grow it, at the end of the day, what it really is, is peace of mind, safety and security to know, okay, the rule of 72, let me look at my return over the next 10 years. And if I can get what we're getting 59% or you can get 29% in the marketplace, if I can do that compared to just putting it in a stock or a bond or something that's safe. And again, nothing against that, but I just saw in 2008, there are so many people I talked to that were getting ready to retire and lost half of their retirement.

And again, I'm not going to tell you I can predict the next five minutes. But as you're saying, over the last 50 years, if you look at real estate the way we're doing it, even if the market dips, right, a rental house or these options we're talking about, even when the market dips a little bit, you don't sell in the downturn. We're holding these things as a five to 10 year hold. Cash flow actually goes up because rents go up when the market dips. And we're holding that thing over a 10 year period.

So from my very biased opinion and my experience, I get real concerned when people have a hundred percent of their money deployed into the stock market. If it's been up for 20 years, we know there's going to be a dip and it's impossible to time the market, which is why I as a control freak a little bit like real estate. Cause I do all my measurements up front, check the market, check what I'm doing. And then once I deploy my capital, I know I'm good for the next five to 10 years. I'm not worried about five months. Could you explain what a cap rate is and why it matters when evaluating a deal? Cause this is real estate basics, basic language, but you got to learn the jargon.

**Dave:** Yeah, that's a great question. So cap rate is a commercial term and it's just one way to measure return and it's a very quick way okay let me size up this deal. And long story short, it's just taking the gross amount of income against how much you invested. So a hundred thousand dollars, if you got a hundred thousand dollar return on something that was a million dollars, that's a ten percent cap rate. So it's just some way that somebody in very quick, ten seconds can say, okay, cap rate is this.

But there's other things like cash on cash return, which is okay, how much money did I put in, right? So the same million-dollar property, if I didn't buy it cash for a million dollars and I leveraged it and I only put $100,000 of my own money and I got the bank to put in $900,000, now we're talking cash on cash because we're only measuring how much cash I put in. That's a different measurement. So cap rate's a very quick, easy way for people to look at a commercial or a real estate investment and say, okay, cap rate's three, cap rate's 10, and size it up at a high level whether it's going to fit inside of their buy bucket.
